issues.title: [A11y] Unify Talkback and voice over Textfield announcements
comments: That's not why you buy an android, to have the ubiquities of iOS on android. I personally am against something like that.

To clarify, the goal is not try to make android to act like ios. it is more about bring flutter Android experience on par with flutter iOS

As of today, both native Android and native iOS can set string to be announce before and after the role announcement.

That is not the case for flutter Android since both semantics label and hint will be announce after role announcement, and this issue is to make sure developer can also set string to be announce before the role announcement.

And since we do this, we should make them more predictable across platforms, that is why i use the word unify in this issue title.

Whenever a developer can have a choice it's good, but it's not good to impose the one and only right way to do things.
Because in that case, let's try to think about unifying it for the other platforms, that is, for windows and Linux, because on Osx it is similar to iOS.
Surely users won't mind and won't wonder why in one app it tells them something this way and in another it tells them something different, and I say this as a blind user and app developer.

> Surely users won't mind and won't wonder why in one app it tells them something this way and in another it tells them something different, and I say this as a blind user and app developer.

If there is a built-in behavior, we definitely should follow it even if it differ between platform.

This is not the case here. In the native Android or other platform, nothing will be announce besides value and role in a native textfield. Native textfield provide API to set to set additional message to be announced. so developer is in full control in how they want something to be pronounced around the value and role announcement.

It is as you said, we only provide the API, it will be up to the developer on what they want to set. If they want to have different announcement for different platform, they can definitely do it by assigning different string based on defaultTargetPlatform

issues.title: Resolve Text Input Code Divergence
issues.body:
I was looking to use `CupertinoTextField` on iOS and `TextField` on Android for my app. The API differences between the two widgets for seemingly similar concepts feel rather unnecessary. Here are some examples:

- To add a piece of hint text, I need to supply that through the `decoration` property on `TextField` but the `placeholder` property on `CupertinoTextField`. 
- The `decoration` property on `TextField` takes an `InputDecoration`, but the same property on `CupertinoTextField` takes a `BoxDecoration`.
- To add a prefix icon, I can use the `prefixIcon` property on `InputDecoration`, which I then pass to the `decoration` property on `TextField`. However, I need to use the `prefix` property on `CupertinoTextField` to achieve a similar result. 

Such differences make it difficult to 1) reason about how to use `CupertinoTextField` based on my knowledge about `TextField`, and 2) minimize platform-specific code when showing one of the two widgets based on the OS. 

It could be helpful if a base text field is provided and design language is applied through theming or decoration.
comments:
CC @justinmc @MitchellGoodwin 
This is an impediment to writing portable code that's definitely worth investigating. Creating support for `TextField.adaptive()` would be non-trivial but perpetuating an incompatible API for Cupertino textfields creates an obstacle for many cross-platform apps. We will investigate.

Thanks for taking a look @HansMuller. 

Here's some food for thought. I was able to customize the M3 TextField widget into something quite close to `CupertinoTextField` in appearance. Here's a demo in DartPad: https://dartpad.dev/?id=5e21d29ef2dc94ddad0a4a635679cfad. As an app developer, I'm not sure if I'd choose this approach over using `CupertinoTextField`. As it stands today, I'd be writing platform specific code either way. However, if this kind of customization came with the Material library, then it can provide some convenience.

I think this has become an even bigger issue with the spellcheck work. On iOS, TextField is not really usable with spellcheck, because of the way the word suggestions are displayed. It's very different from the expected behavior and when I tested it, it overflows. 
![Apr-07-2023 13-20-18](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/21065911/230650530-e89c64ed-a6fe-4098-8f09-2ff315047dd1.gif)

@camsim99 Is there a GitHub issue for the bug posted by @leighajarett above?

*Edit*: It looks like two things: 1. we should handle overflow and 2. TextField on iOS should still show the iOS-style context menu by default.

I'm super interested in this greater idea by @InMatrix to build iOS-looking widgets in Material.  I'm definitely on board with helping to support that and/or trying to synchronize the APIs for TextField and CupertinoTextField more.

I agree with @justinmc. While there may be value to having distinct Cupertino widgets, that's only if both Cupertino and Material see equal amounts of support.

Currently, Material widgets keep getting new API features and better accessibility updates that very slowly (if ever) trickle down to the Cupertino versions.

If the distinctions are mostly visual styling, it might make more sense to have the Cupertino version be a child of the Material widget.

The primary distinction that I can currently think of is that Cupertino widgets use the `CupertinoTheme` (which like the widgets has lagged behind the Material counterpart... especially with respect to dark mode). With respect to the `CupertinoTheme`, wouldn't it possibly make more sense to provide a `ColorScheme` that replicates the Cupertino light and dark colors?
FYI @dkwingsmt for your work on the Cupertino API. It's probably already on your radar, but it would be nice to make the TextField and CupertinoTextField APIs match more closely.

issues.title: [macOS] TextField doesn't receive text from apps that simulate keyboard events (e.g. MacWhisper dictation)
issues.body:
## Steps to reproduce

1. Create a minimal Flutter app with a `TextField` on macOS (see sample below)
2. Install [MacWhisper](https://goodsnooze.gumroad.com/l/macwhisper) and enable its Dictation feature
3. Focus the Flutter `TextField`
4. Use MacWhisper dictation to speak text
5. **No text appears** in the TextField

## Expected behavior

Text should appear in the TextField, as it does in native macOS apps (TextEdit, Safari, Notes, etc.).

## Actual behavior

No text appears at all. The TextField remains empty.

## Additional context

- **macOS built-in dictation** (Fn+Fn / Globe key) **works correctly** in Flutter TextFields
- MacWhisper dictation **works correctly** in native macOS apps (TextEdit, Safari, etc.)
- This also reproduces with a **vanilla Flutter project** — no custom text input handling needed

This suggests that macOS built-in dictation uses the `NSTextInputClient` protocol directly (`insertText:replacementRange:`), which Flutter's `FlutterTextInputPlugin` handles. MacWhisper likely uses CGEvent keyboard simulation, which native `NSTextField`/`NSTextView` apps process through the standard NSResponder chain, but Flutter's text input plugin does not.

## Related issues

- #98742 — Simulated key events don't produce text input on desktop platforms

## Sample code

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() => runApp(const MaterialApp(home: TestPage()));

class TestPage extends StatelessWidget {
  const TestPage({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(title: const Text('MacWhisper Test')),
      body: const Padding(
        padding: EdgeInsets.all(24),
        child: Column(
          children: [
            Text('Focus the text field and use MacWhisper Dictation:'),
            SizedBox(height: 16),
            TextField(
              decoration: InputDecoration(
                border: OutlineInputBorder(),
                labelText: 'Dictate here...',
              ),
            ),
          ],
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

## Flutter version

```
Flutter 3.41.2 • channel stable
Framework • revision 90673a4eef (2026-02-18)
Engine • revision 6c0baaebf7
```

## macOS version

Darwin 25.1.0 (macOS Tahoe)
comments: @friebetill Thanks for the report.
I am a bit confused here. We pass keyboard events into `NSTextInputContext`, which turns them into IME actions.
Moving this to team-macos as I'm guessing the solution lies in the macOS embedder.
I believe this is caused by https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/178609

this is a real pain point for anyone building voice-to-text tools on macOS. the core issue is that Flutter's FlutterTextInputPlugin only handles NSTextInputClient protocol messages (insertText:replacementRange:), but apps like MacWhisper use CGEvent posts which go through the NSResponder chain that Flutter doesn't hook into.

we hit the exact same thing building a voice-first desktop agent, CGEvent-based typing works in every native app but gets swallowed by custom input handling. our workaround was to detect whether the target app uses a standard NSTextView and fall back to accessibility API insertions (AXUIElement setValue) when CGEvents don't land. not pretty but it works across Flutter, Electron, and other non-native text inputs.

for reference, our macOS automation layer handles this exact CGEvent vs accessibility API fallback. the keyboard simulation and AXUIElement text insertion logic is in: https://github.com/mediar-ai/mcp-server-macos-use/blob/main/Sources/MCPServer/main.swift

and the desktop automation framework that handles the cross-toolkit detection (native vs Electron vs Flutter): https://github.com/mediar-ai/terminator/blob/main/crates/terminator/src/lib.rs

In AngularDart they got rid of async validators because they complicated the implementation a lot.
I don't know in detail how they solved it in the end, but it's something like invoking sync validation again when the response of the async request becomes available.
Also pretty keen on this idea @eseidelGoogle and @Hixie. Would be nice to be able to do things like check if a username already exists on a sign up page in the field validation.

+1 
any known workarounds?

What @zoechi suggests seems to be working for me. 

Implement a validator that is conditional on local state. After submitting the form and the async call returns a problem related to a form field, set the local state to enable the related conditional clause in the validator to display the related error message.

To explicitly run the validators when the form reloads use:
```
_formKey.currentState?.validate();
```
This should display the related error message for the problem form field after the async call.

Hello, you can use [flutter_form_bloc](https://pub.dev/packages/flutter_form_bloc). It also has debounce time property.
Check out the [example](https://giancarlocode.github.io/form_bloc/#/async_field_validation_example)

Adding built-in support for an async validator will increase the complexity of our form fields, but it's clearly something lots of people need to do.  It could work out if we come up with a good implementation.

This would be a nice feature to be natively in flutter.

It would be a breaking change of course, because the `FormState` validate function have to be of type `Future<bool> validate()`. But we also want to preserve the sync validation, so this should not be a breaking change.

In my opinion, the best solution would be to preserve current `Form` and form fields and add another widgets and classes that will be for the async form validation e.g `AsyncForm`, `AsyncTextFormField`, `AsyncFormState`.

This would **not be a breaking change** and would be really nice addition 🥇

issues.title: iOS obscureText input security features
issues.body:
## Use case

It seems that since probably iOS 13+ the system actively protects users from showing obscured text like passwords and related stuff like keyboards in screenshots, recordings, screen sharing etc. I could not find some official paper with specifications, but from testing and sources like https://www.reddit.com/r/iphone/comments/eqox55/if_you_go_into_your_passwords_and_try_to/ (screenshot), https://discussions.apple.com/thread/251035283 (video), https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/116777, etc. I figured out:

for screenshots, recording, screen sharing on iOS the apps while obscureText=true:
1. hide keyboards
2. hide content of obscureText (passwords) inputs
3. immediately when the state of the input changes (aka user clicked on "eye" icon etc), it switches

This works for all native apps I tested including settings, Twitter, Instagram, ...

It seems that Flutter only supports the 1. thing, aka keyboard is hidden while.

## Proposal

Explore more about these features, implement hiding of text field content on these occasions and propagate the changes of obscureText.
comments: Thanks for pointing this out.  Can you clarify what exactly is happen in Flutter for points 2 and 3 versus what should be happening?  Also, are you saying that this is only during screenshot/recording/screensharing and not during normal app use?

Hi, if you take a look at native apps, as I mentioned, they hide password content and length + keyboards while obscured text, and they show the content + keyboards while the input is not obscured.

In Flutter (while recording, screenshot, probably screen share, ...), once textfield is obscured, keyboard is always hidden (no matter if obscuredText changes). The password input content switching work in a way that if I switch obscuredText to false, the content is visible as plain text, if I change it to true, it shows replacement character for it while in native it doesn't show anything.  

Take a look at a video comparing Twitter and Flutter (red color is color of Scaffold, yellow color is color of its content/Column).

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5287596/213691262-4fc03ef7-a3e7-4eb9-b970-5b3bf50ae36b.MOV

I still could not find any proper documentation of this feature by Apple, but some more sources:

- https://discussions.apple.com/thread/253402530
- https://discussions.apple.com/thread/251479386
- https://discussions.apple.com/thread/251549153
cc @LongCatIsLooong https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/18643

> Hi, if you take a look at native apps, as I mentioned, they hide password content and length + keyboards while obscured text, and they show the content + keyboards while the input is not obscured.
> 
> In Flutter (while recording, screenshot, probably screen share, ...), once textfield is obscured, keyboard is always hidden (no matter if obscuredText changes). The password input content switching work in a way that if I switch obscuredText to false, the content is visible as plain text, if I change it to true, it shows replacement character for it while in native it doesn't show anything.
> 
> Take a look at a video comparing Twitter and Flutter (red color is color of Scaffold, yellow color is color of its content/Column).
> 
>  Upload.from.GitHub.for.iOS.MOV 
> I still could not find any proper documentation of this feature by Apple, but some more sources:
> 
> * https://discussions.apple.com/thread/253402530
> * https://discussions.apple.com/thread/251479386
> * https://discussions.apple.com/thread/251549153

Have your issue solved?

issues.title: TextFields (and TextFormFields) should allow displaying error state without error text
issues.body:
<!-- Thank you for using Flutter!

     If you are looking for support, please check out our documentation
     or consider asking a question on Stack Overflow:
      * https://flutter.dev/
      * https://api.flutter.dev/
      * https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/flutter?sort=frequent

     If you have found a bug or if our documentation doesn't have an answer
     to what you're looking for, then fill our the template below. Please read
     our guide to filing a bug first: https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports
-->

## Use case

There are occasions where multiple `TextField` (or more likely `TextFormField`) are used on a page and the UX could call for a custom error solution instead of each textfield displaying an error message. We want to maintain the validate logic that exists on the Form but allow the developer to decide whether each textfield should show its error text.

## Proposal

Adding a new parameter to `InputDecoration` that tells TextFields they should not display their error text but still display any other error specific content (errorBorder, etc)

This would have to also work with the validate logic of `TextFormField` as well so that if a form is validated the error text can choose to not be displayed on each text field.

cc @justinmc 
Similar to https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/52634
comments:
This is something I do too! Right now this is how I do it:
```dart
decoration: InputDecoration(
  counterStyle: const TextStyle(height: 0),
  counterText: ' ',
  errorStyle: const TextStyle(height: 0),
),
```
And then error for the validator is simply an empty string. So when there is an error all that is shown is the red error line.
yup I'm using this workaround for now as well but it would be nice to have a more formal way to support this.
The workaround from @MichaelPriebe has a problem: it causes an empty space to appear under the textfield (where the error used to be). I would be nice to have a solution without this space.

> The workaround from @MichaelPriebe has a problem: it causes an empty space to appear under the textfield (where the error used to be). I would be nice to have a solution without this space.

Try this: `TextStyle(height: 0.01)`, I get it working here with master.

> > The workaround from @MichaelPriebe has a problem: it causes an empty space to appear under the textfield (where the error used to be). I would be nice to have a solution without this space.
> 
> Try this: `TextStyle(height: 0.01)`, I get it working here with master.

Thank you this worked by using 0.001 however this is a very ugly solution.
We need a real solution to lose error text and prevent text field outline getting shorter height.
It is crazy such a simple thing is not still solved.

Thanks! using `TextStyle(height: 0.001)` worked to hide the error message.

It would be nice to be able to decide how to show the error message from the different options:
- not showing this at all
- showing it under the textform
- showing the error text as tooltip.

Similar problem here on web platform: 
![immagine](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/44212022/216346835-a499416b-cf97-49d0-abbc-a47ffef5af1c.png)
The `TextFormField` on the left is using the "0.01" workaround, and the one on the right is using the `TextStyle(height: 0)` solution (which I'm pretty sure it worked fine some time ago, as I used it in another project of mine)
In conjunction with this, an errorLabelTextStyle property seems reasonable.

issues.title: Allow more control of TextInputControls in EditableText
issues.body:
### Use case

Some apps need the option of using their own keyboard for security reasons. You often do not want other software or hardware keyboards to be able to track what is currently being entered in a text field.
Possible use cases are, for example, banking apps or other apps that have to meet certain security requirements.

We are currently unable to publish our Flutter app because a requirement for this is that external input is not allowed. The only possibility at the moment is to implement `EditableText` and `TextField`/`TextFormField` ourselves.

### Proposal

It could be made possible for `EditableTexts` or `TextFields` to control which `TextInputs` can modify them or receive updates from `TextEditingValues` or similar. For example, you could give each `TextInputConnection` the option of defining its own filter function for all permitted `TextInputControls`. Alternatively you could at least provide the option to prohibit external `TextInputs` such as `_PlatformTextInputControl.instance`.
comments: To use in-app keyboards, you can disable software keyboard using [`TextInputType.none`](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/services/TextInputType/none-constant.html). Does that work for you?

> To use in-app keyboards, you can disable software keyboard using [`TextInputType.none`](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/services/TextInputType/none-constant.html). Does that work for you?

It's not primarily about using your own keyboard. You can do that relatively easily. Nevertheless, in this case the keyboard of the operating system is informed of every change, which is a security vulnerability that is sometimes not allowed. 

In the following two places, each connected keyboard is informed of a change regardless of the `TextInputType`. It would therefore be useful if you could somehow control which `TextInputControls` are allowed to access a connection.

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/86fc1fd47951eab6031c1c7e602d8499bf33216e/packages/flutter/lib/src/services/text_input.dart#L1991-L1995

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/86fc1fd47951eab6031c1c7e602d8499bf33216e/packages/flutter/lib/src/services/text_input.dart#L2057-L2068

It looks like the platform text input control is considered "special" atm: 

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/86fc1fd47951eab6031c1c7e602d8499bf33216e/packages/flutter/lib/src/services/text_input.dart#L1649-L1659

It seems to make sense to be able to remove them as well. But since you want to prevent communication between the text field & the on-screen / physical keyboard, does it work if you set the text field to readOnly (which prevents the text field from establish any text input connections) and use the TextEditingController to control the input? I haven't tried that and things like blinking cursors may not work.

> I haven't tried that and things like blinking cursors may not work.

@LongCatIsLooong Yes, this does not work for the reason you mentioned: The cursor is not displayed correctly. Even if it would work, it would still be useful if you could implement this use case without self-implementation that bypasses `TextInput` with a `TextEditingController` .

There are also other reasons why custom virtual keyboards do not work with `EditableText`. For example, scrolling to the current `EditableText` only works with the native keyboard, as scrolling is based on `View.of(context)` here.

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/86fc1fd47951eab6031c1c7e602d8499bf33216e/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/editable_text.dart#L4133-L4150

So it's generally difficult to use your own keyboards in Flutter at the moment.

issues.title: TextField with `canRequestFocus: false` has inconsistent behavior across platforms
issues.body:
## Background [**TextField**](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/TextField-class.html) has several boolean flags that share similar but distinct purposes. - Setting [**enabled**](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/TextField/enabled.html) to `false` grays it out, and it doesn't respond to any attempt to tap or scroll the text. - When [**readOnly**](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/TextField/readOnly.html) is `true`, the text can be scrolled and selected, but not edited. - When [**canRequestFocus**](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/TextField/canRequestFocus.html) is `false`, the text can be scrolled but not focused. Here's a [**DartPad sample**](https://dartpad.dev/?id=e334b204aaf8b3887005fe4a5c9aa48d) that shows each property in action.
## Problem ### Web When using `canRequestFocus: false`, the text field can scroll but no other type of interaction is possible. Setting `readOnly` as `true` or `false` doesn't change any behavior. ### Windows Using `canRequestFocus: false` still opens a context menu when you right-click. If you "paste" it will paste whatever is in your clipboard at that spot, and clicking "select all" scrolls to the bottom with no other visual change. Using `canRequestFocus: false, readOnly: true` gets rid of the "paste" option, but "select all" acts the same way as above. ### Android Using `canRequestFocus: false` shows the selection handles but not any highlighting. The only thing changed by `readOnly` is whether or not a "paste" button shows up. ### MacOS / iOS I haven't tried this out on Apple platforms but would be interested to see what happens.
## Proposal The intuition I've gained from running the sample on web is that `canRequestFocus: false` conveys "can scroll but can't focus, edit, or select". I would love if this intuition applied to all platforms. Specifically, on Windows there shouldn't be a right-click context menu and on Android there shouldn't be any weird selection handles. If this proposal sounds good to everybody, I can open a PR to implement a fix, though I imagine it would have to wait until after the decoupling mentioned in #184093 is finished.
comments:
Hiw @nate-thegrate .

Thanks for reporting this and providing a minimal code sample. I'm routing this to team-framework to evaluate the proposal. I'm also sharing my results when running on macOS (which are similar to Windows)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/84f4e528-8892-4a14-8ae0-29a616ce79df

Thanks for flagging this. I agree this will have to happen after decoupling, but we should get this right.

I wonder why the behavior is different per platform. That might make sense on web but I would expect the other platforms to behave in the same way unless we are deliberately doing different things on each platform. @nate-thegrate have you looked at the code that causes these differences?

Overall I'm on board with normalizing on the web behavior as you suggested. Android looks broken when it doesn't show selection, and Windows probably shouldn't allow paste. Web's behavior seems reasonable.

> @nate-thegrate have you looked at the code that causes these differences?

I haven't yet! I'll plan on doing a deep dive once the decoupling is complete.

issues.title: [ios]Support secure paste for iPad hardware keyboard shortcut (CMD+V)
issues.body:
## Problem: 

When user type CMD+V, Flutter app shows paste warning, but native app does not show the warning. (related: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/103163)

## Why flutter apps trigger this warning?

The path is quite complex with multiple steps: 
1. User press CMD+V
2. iOS calls `FlutterTextInputView`'s key press [event handlers](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/23fc488a6c33b7633ecff4b27713782fc4dcc640/shell/platform/darwin/ios/framework/Source/FlutterTextInputPlugin.mm#L2219-L2238)  
3. The keyboard event is [eventually relayed to FlutterKeyboardManager](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/23fc488a6c33b7633ecff4b27713782fc4dcc640/shell/platform/darwin/ios/framework/Source/FlutterViewController.mm#L1863), which sends to the framework
4. Framework handles the keyboard event and [request access to clipboard](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/2d422a326120714355d06aa713f809c9aea84651/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/editable_text.dart#L2483) from the engine
5. FlutterPlatformPlugin [programmatically access the pasteboard](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/23fc488a6c33b7633ecff4b27713782fc4dcc640/shell/platform/darwin/ios/framework/Source/FlutterPlatformPlugin.mm#L379), triggering the paste warning

## Why native apps are ok?
As explained in [the design doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/16-8kn58h_oD902e7vPSh6W20aHRBJKyNOdSe5rbAe_g/edit?resourcekey=0-gVdJ3fbOybV70ZKeHU7fkQ&tab=t.0), iOS has access to private APIs, which contains the secret logic that unlocks the pasteboard content. That logic is probably invoked by the default keyboard handler (which we override). 

## Solution? 

To find out where this logic is, I removed the custom handlers from both `FlutterTextInputView` and `FlutterViewController`. By doing so, iOS will just follows the responder chain and eventually use default handlers, which unlocks the pasteboard contents. Then I was able to successfully paste in content using keyboard, without triggering the paste warning. 

So problem solved? Not quite! 

Removing these custom handlers will probably break framework's [KeyboardListener widget](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/KeyboardListener-class.html). I am not familiar with how keyboard works, so this will need more investigation to properly solve it (if it's solvable). I don't think it's high priority, as it only impact iPad hardware keyboard.
comments: This used to work before pencil changes some time ago

@moffatman Really? I wonder if it's because that change started returning NO from canPerformAction ([code](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/fbc9b889aee912d74e73f55a15565c7e7125cc31/shell/platform/darwin/ios/framework/Source/FlutterTextInputPlugin.mm#L1148-L1152)).

@hellohuanlin that sort of solution might at the very least break developers' ability to override the cmd-V keyboard shortcut in their Dart code.

I think just fixing canPerformAction will be good on its own, it will enable use of the shortcut actions

It looks like Flutter might consume the key event. I see that the TextInputPlugin is a secondary responder to keyboard events ([code](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/d5d7526b117dc76ac1cf851ca370be9284ece351/shell/platform/darwin/ios/framework/Source/FlutterViewController.mm#L688)). I'd have to try it though.
